div.p: Alexander, by the grace of God king of the Scots, to all worthy men of his entire land, greeting. Know us to have inspected the writing of agreements made at Berwick in our presence between Patrick, earl of Dunbar, our nephew, on the one side, and the abbot and convent of Melrose on the other, about a land in the territory of Hassington which Master William of Greenlaw gave in the full court of the said Earl of Dunbar to the said abbot and convent as well as to their assigns, drawn up in these words: To all Christ's faithful who shall view or hear this writing, Patrick, earl of Dunbar, sends greeting. Let everyone present and future know that, since Master William of Greenlaw had solemnly given in our court at Edrom, to the abbot and convent of Melrose as well as to their assigns, a certain land that he had from the gift of Robert de Muschamp our tenant in the feu of Hassington, donated to him and his heirs and assigns, and since the same Master William had put himself out of possession of the same land in that place and solemnly put them in possession of the same, so that the possession of the abbot and convent is peaceably and perpetually established for future times, between them on the one side and us on the other, in the presence of our well-beloved lord Alexander, illustrious king of the Scots, and his barons; it was agreed that we promise, grant, and by this our present charter bind ourselves and our heirs, that we would acquit, warrant, and defend for ever the said holding to the abbot and convent against all people. For the same abbot and convent to hold and to have in free and perpetual alms, by the boundaries by which the said Master William held it and with all pertinents, liberties, and easements pertaining to it, and with common pasture of the toun of Hassington, and with free entry and exit to the same pasture and to the said easments, as wholly, freely, quitly, and honourably from every secular service, exaction, and demand, just as any alms in the kingdom of Scotland is most freely, quitly, fully, and honourably held and possessed. And if anyone move a claim against the said Abbot and convent of Melrose concerning the foregoing, and we or our heirs do not defended them, so that it becomes necessary for them to lose the said land, or be ejected from it by judgment; we or our heirs shall give the reasonable value to the claimant in our demesne or elsewhere in a suitable place to the value of the said land; and so we shall make satisfaction to the same in everything, that the said abbot and convent of Melrose shall retain and possess the said land which Master William of Greenlaw conveyed to them free and quit, peaceably and continuously forever. We, however, in order to do the aforesaid things, for the sake of ourselves and our heirs have provided a bodily oath, and we have the more fully obliged ourselves and our heirs, submitting ourselves and our heirs to the jurisdiction of the Bishop of Glasgow -- whoever it shall be at the time -- so that besides the royal power, by which we ought and wish to be constrained, the said bishop can compel us and our heirs by ecclesiastical censure, with every legal remedy removed and without appeal, to the observance of all the aforesaid things. And so that this writing retain authority and strength, we have caused it to be authenticated by the application of our seal. Enacted at Berwick on the fourteenth day of April in the thirty-fourth year of the reign of the lord king, Alexander. As witnesses, Master Gamelin, the lords Robert of Roos, David of Graham, Robert of Menzies, William fitz Count, Thomas de Normanville, John Comyn, Henry of Graham, Nicholas Corbet, Walter son of Walter the steward, Robert fitz Count, Thomas del Bois, Bernard of Ripley, and many others. At the request of the same Earl Patrick, the abbot and convent, we have therefore licensed the aforesaid agreements and made them firm by our present charter and royal authority. As witnesses, William earl of Mar, Alexander the steward, Robert of Roos, William of Lindsay, William Oliphant, Nicholas de Soulis, William our chaplain, Alexander the clerk of the chapel, Thomas the clerk of the livery, and others. At Berwick on the twentieth day of April in the thirty-fourth year of the reign of the lord king.
